schools.summary: Chrysostom: the oil is charity and alms, the lamps the gift of virginity; the foolish lost the greater for the less, lacking works of mercy.
Augustine: the oil is inward joy in pleasing God (Psalm 45:7); Jerome: good works added to faith, the parable of the whole human race; Gregory: the Church, wise mixed with foolish.
Origen: the oil is the word of teaching, the lamps the natural faculties; the foolish profess faith without good works.
Henry: lamps = profession (children of light, Philippians 2:15), oil = inward grace derived from Christ through the ordinances (Zechariah 4; John 1:16).
Lightfoot on the Jewish wedding custom of ten; the marriage-feast imagery of the bridegroom Christ.

sides.position: Inward joy / good works added to faith
The word of teaching / sound doctrine
Inward grace, the principle of a living faith

outliers.note: Reads the parable of the whole human race, not virgins only, the ten as the five senses doubled.

schools.summary: Henry: the same profession may hide vastly different states; hypocrisy the worst folly; Tillotson's 'charitable decorum' in the even number, yet the gate is strait.
Wisdom and folly measured wholly by provision — the wise foresaw the delay, the foolish did not.
Calvin: profession is no proof of grace, the parable's abiding warning.
Henry: the foolish have no inward principle (the stony ground) and make no provision for what is to come — a show for men, not approval before Christ.
They had oil to light the lamp 'for the present' but no reserve against delay — the very contingency the parable introduces.
Henry: the heart is the vessel, grace the oil (Exodus 35:14); the wise lay in a reserve against delay, the inward principle of faith and love behind the shining lamp.
The wise carried a reserve so their lamps would not fail whatever the delay.
Jerome and the fathers: the sleep is death ('slumbered' = died, 'slept' = to be wakened); the tarrying = the interval between the two comings; Christ comes at midnight (Exodus 12:29).
Henry: the sleep is spiritual remissness, not death; the delay tests patience (Habakkuk 2:3); slumber leads to sleep — dread the beginning of decays.
Calvin: the tarrying is the long interval that tests profession.
Wesley keeps the plain call to watchfulness.

debates.question: What does the slumbering and sleeping signify?

sides.position: The death of all before the resurrection
Spiritual remissness creeping over even waiting saints

schools.summary: Henry: though slow he is sure (Habakkuk 2:3); he comes at our midnight, least expected (Exodus 12:29; Luke 12:20); we must go forth to meet him; the cry is awakening, every eye shall see him.
Origen: the midnight cry is the angels arousing all who sleep; all hear and rise, but not all can trim their lamps fitly.
Jerome: midnight is the hour of deepest security, the coming proclaimed by the shout of angels and trumpets.
Henry: in the wise, actual preparation for death (2 Peter 3:14; Matthew 24:46); in the foolish, vain confidence — counterfeit graces blazing like a lightning before death.
All alike trimmed their lamps and looked the same for a moment, until the foolish lamps gutter for want of oil.
Henry: the hypocrite's lamp goes out (2 Peter 2:20; Job 27:8); at the last the carnal would gladly be found Christians, but the request comes too late — no buying when the market is over.
The foolish, awakened, find their lamps failing and can only beg what cannot be borrowed.
Jerome: the wise refuse not from covetousness but fear — each receives his own works' reward, the virtues of one cannot atone for another's vices; the oil costs much toil.
Henry: each must have grace of his own; the most have none to spare — against Rome's supererogation and imputed merits of saints.
Chrysostom: none can be patron of another's failure at the judgment; the poor are the merchants, so store up oil here and now.
Calvin: grace is not transferable; the time to obtain it is now.
Chrysostom: 'while they went to buy' shows mercy after death avails nothing (Luke 16); the time for oil is now.
Jerome and Augustine: the season for buying past, no room for penitence; the shut door = the close of all entrance after the judgment.
The ready went in, the unready were shut out; the door once shut is shut for ever — the end of probation.
The doubled 'Lord, Lord' echoes Matthew 7:21-22 — the foolish call on the name they slighted, but cannot open what negligence has shut.
The Gloss: they say 'Lord' not 'Father,' whose mercy they despised in life; they can claim his lordship but not his fatherhood.
Jerome: confessing 'Lord' with the mouth while denying with works avails nothing; the Lord knows his own (2 Timothy 2:19); without oil they are unknown by the bridegroom.
'I know you not' = judicial non-acknowledgement, not ignorance — to be 'known' of God is to be approved; this is to be cast off.
Cyril of Jerusalem turns it to exhortation: wash your robes in repentance lest you hear 'I know you not.'
Jerome: the moral is to provide the light of good works since the day is unknown; Augustine: ready for our own falling asleep, we are ready for his coming.
The ignorance of the time is given to keep us always prepared — habitual readiness, not date-setting.

Gregory: the journey = Christ ascending in the flesh; the talents = senses, understanding, action; Jerome: the diversity of gospel gifts (1 Cor 3:2); Hilary allegorises Jews, Gentiles, and Law-keepers.
Origen: Christ travels not in his divine nature (which is ever present, Matthew 28:20) but in the dispensation of the flesh.
Henry: the master is Christ the Owner, the servants Christians (especially ministers), the talents his rich gifts given by ability (Ephesians 4:8) for working, not idleness.
The plain frame: gifts entrusted to be used, distributed according to capacity.

outliers.note: Allegorises the three servants as believing Jews, Gentiles, and Law-bound Jews who hide the talent through envy of Gentile salvation.

schools.summary: Origen: the measures are degrees of capacity; even one talent of such a master is great; the five mapped to deep scriptural insight, the one to the less strong.
Henry: God divides as he will, fitting gifts to ability yet giving the ability too; every one has at least one talent (a soul), none has all alike.
Lightfoot and Barnes on the value of a talent and the custom.
Calvin/Wesley: the diversity and justice of God's distribution — none empty, each judged by what he received.
Henry: the Christian is a spiritual tradesman trading with his Master's stock (Proverbs 3:15; Matthew 13:45); prompt diligence improving every gift and ordinance, the diligent hand made rich.
The servant put the trust to immediate, profitable use, the increase proportioned to the capital.
Henry: the man of two doubled his trust as truly as the man of five; the Lord expects improvement proportioned to the gift, encouraging those of lesser sphere.
The servant of two traded with the same fidelity, gaining a proportionate increase — faithfulness, not size, is the measure.
Henry: of ten lepers nine hid the talent (Luke 17:17-18); many with five talents bury them all — the unfaithful does no positive evil but no good.
The hidden talent = the gift unused, withheld from service; the fault is non-use, not riot.
Jerome: 'a long time' = the interval between the ascension and the second coming.
Origen: the delay is the time of sending laborers; the Lord comes to the servants, not they to him, when the time is accomplished.
Chrysostom: the deferred reckoning shows the Lord's long-suffering, alluding to the resurrection.
The master returns and every servant renders account — the reckoning sure however long delayed.
The good servant reports the increase humbly, owning the trust as his Lord's ('thou deliveredst'); the account open and complete, five received, five gained.
Calvin: the servant comes with boldness yet without boasting, the doubling the return on the Lord's stock.

Raban: 'Well done' an interjection of joy (Psalm 16:11); Gregory: set over many things = eternal joy with no room for sorrow; Jerome: faithful in a few, for present things are few beside the coming; Augustine: perfect joy is fruition of the Trinity.
Chrysostom: 'good' of goodness toward one's neighbour; the 'joy' = complete blessedness.
The threefold reward: approval, advancement, and admission to the Lord's own joy — communion with the Master.
Gill: the two-talent servant renders the same humble account as the first, owning the trust and bringing the doubled increase.
Jerome (on v.21): the master looks to the will, not the largeness of profit, receiving both with equal favour.
Gill: the same commendation, advancement, and joy given to the two-talent servant as to the five — reward measured by faithfulness, not gain.
Cyril of Jerusalem cites the words as the blessing the faithful long to hear.
Origen: the slothful servant believes but acts dishonestly, fearing God as austere; the Lord is 'hard' only to those who abuse his mercy into remissness.
Jerome: the excuse adds pride to sloth — he cavils against his Lord instead of confessing, turning negligence into accusation.
Calvin/Wesley/the expositors: he blames the Lord's supposed harshness for his own inaction, as though the fault lay with the giver.

outliers.note: Diagnoses the servant as one who believes but hides his faith, regarding God as austere and implacable.

schools.summary: The servant's fear is the cover for sloth; returning the talent intact is not faithfulness, for it was given to be traded, not kept.
Wesley keeps the plain sense: the boast of safe-keeping is the proof of unprofitableness.
Jerome: 'wicked' for cavilling against his Lord, 'slothful' for not doubling the talent — pride and idleness both condemned.
The master argues from the servant's own premise: even granting it, his conduct is inexcusable — the excuse supplies the condemnation.
Jerome: the money is the gospel word (Psalm 12:6), to be given to the bankers — other doctors or believers — who double it by acting what they learned.
The master argues on the servant's own terms — even fear should have prompted the minimal increase of lending at interest; Lightfoot on the interest-paying bankers.
Origen: the least diligence would have sufficed, and the servant rendered not even that.
The talent is transferred, not destroyed — taken from the one who buried it, given to the most faithful, so the gift passes to where it is used.
Gregory: the talent goes to the diligent because gifts gravitate toward faithfulness, as the faithful in outward things grow mighty in inward understanding.
Gregory: charity gains all gifts, its lack loses all; Jerome: faith and right will are increased, faithlessness loses even natural virtues, for what is unused 'seemed' to be his.
The plain law: gifts used are increased, gifts unused forfeited — 'having' is faithful use, not bare possession.
Calvin/Wesley: the principle justifying the talent's transfer — grace exercised multiplies, refused is withdrawn.
Chrysostom: not only the robber and evildoer but he who does no good works is punished — the servant cast out for mere omission.
Origen: 'outer darkness' is the place where God is not seen, the condemned counted unworthy of the contemplation of God — privation as well as pain.
The plain sentence: the unprofitable servant condemned, the weeping and gnashing the anguish of the lost — no neutral ground in the kingdom.
Chrysostom: no longer 'the kingdom is like' but direct revelation — the Son of man comes in majesty with the angels as witnesses to their ministry of salvation.
Augustine: he judges in the human form he took from us, seen as God only afterward; Remigius: 'majesty' = his divinity, equal to the Father; Raban/Gregory: the manner of the judgment to come.
Origen: the coming in transfiguration-glory; the throne as the perfect saints or angelic powers (Colossians 1:16).
Henry: judgment committed to the Son of man (Acts 17:31), his appearing splendid, in the Father's and his own mediatorial glory, the angels ministers of justice, the throne of judgment (Daniel 7:9).
Henry: a general judgment of all ages and places; the separation so exact that no saint is lost in the crowd nor sinner hidden (Malachi 3:18; Psalm 1:5; Ezekiel 34:17), Christ the great Shepherd.
The godly as sheep (mild, useful), the wicked as goats (baser, unruly), now in one pasture, then in different folds.
Gill: the scene chiefly concerns Christian professors, giving a lively idea of the whole; Calvin on the sorting.
Henry: the right hand honour, the left shame (Daniel 12:2); not rich and poor but godly and wicked, every other distinction abolished, the eternal state fixed by this one.
Origen: the right hand is rest and glory, the left torment; sheep for the meekness learned of Christ (Matthew 11:29; Isaiah 53:7), goats for treading rough and dangerous ways.
Chrysostom: not 'Receive' but 'Inherit/Possess,' as due from of old.
Augustine distinguishes the present Church-kingdom of conflict from the kingdom of peace; Jerome: 'prepared from the foundation' = God's foreknowledge, things to come as already done.
Henry: the King pronounces them blessed and makes them so; blessed of the Father though cursed by the world (Ephesians 1:3); 'Come' a tenfold welcome, the crown after the cross.
The kingdom an inheritance of free grace, 'prepared,' entered by a fruitful faith.
Chrysostom: how light the things enjoined — bread, a visit — yet it is God who receives in the poor man, and what He takes is our own.
Origen: Christ hungers in his body the Church (1 Corinthians 12:27), suffering what it suffers though impassible; the Judge dwells on the righteous' deeds.
The works of mercy are the evidence and fruit of faith and love, not the meritorious cause of salvation.
Gill: the fruits of grace, produced by the Spirit that unites the saints to Christ, not the price of salvation.

outliers.note: Explains the impassible Christ's hunger through the union of head and body — he suffers what his Church suffers.

schools.summary: The six works of mercy completed (Remigius); a representative, not exhaustive, list of love's plain duties toward the suffering.
Chrysostom (on v.35): the lightness of the things enjoined — ready kindness, not heroic feats.
Raban: they answer not from distrust but from amazement at their exaltation and the smallness of their works beside the reward (Romans 8:18).
Origen: humility makes them disclaim their deeds; the righteous dwell on each word, the wicked answer summarily.
The righteous genuinely astonished, having served Christ in his poor without calculating reward or perceiving whom they served.
The righteous rehearse the King's list back as a question, unable to recall ministering to him, having served his poor without thought of reward.
Gill: the disclaiming of their deeds is part of their commendation, love that keeps no accounts.

themes: when saw we thee a stranger

schools.summary: The righteous complete their wondering question, finding no record of ministering to the King himself, having served his afflicted members.
Gill: their unawareness is the seal of the genuineness of their works, free from calculation of reward.
Jerome restricts 'the least my brethren' to the poor in spirit, the doers of the Father's will (Matthew 12:50), not the poor generally.
Chrysostom reads broadly: every believer, even secular, made a brother by baptism, though lowly and outcast.
Origen: mercy to the more holy is more acceptable; the truly 'brethren' are the perfect.
Christ identifies himself with his suffering people; mercy to them is mercy to him, no act of charity lost.

debates.question: Who are 'the least of these my brethren'?

sides.position: Christ's needy disciples / brethren in the faith
The poor and suffering generally (an open reading)

themes: the least of these my brethren

schools.summary: Barnes: the fire 'prepared for the devil,' not for men, who inherit it by sharing his character; Ellicott: 'of my Father' omitted, for God is not the author of the curse.
Clarke: the place was prepared for the devil, never designed for men; the wicked perish not for want of salvation but for refusing it — cursed because they refused to be blessed.
Calvin: the threat is given to restrain believers by fear, that they not envy the reprobate's lot.
Origen: the Judge enlarges on good deeds but cuts short the evil, the brevity itself a mark of mercy.
The wicked condemned for omission — the hungry unfed, the thirsty unrelieved — the exact counterpart of the righteous' works.
Origen: the wicked's failures gathered summarily, answering to the summary excuse they offer, while the righteous humbly disclaim each deed.
The wicked condemned for the good left undone — the negative of the righteous' works, the absence of mercy betraying a heart estranged from Christ.
The wicked answer summarily and self-justifyingly (Origen), protesting they never knowingly refused Christ, not perceiving him in his poor.
Calvin/Gill: their plea is their condemnation — they would have served a Christ who could advance them, despising the lowly members in whom he was present.
Origen: to the righteous 'my brethren' (magnifying their deeds), to the wicked only 'one of the least' (not aggravating their sin) — restraint even in condemnation.
The exact negative of v.40: mercy withheld from the least is withheld from Christ, who was present all along in his needy people.

themes: ye did it not to me

schools.summary: Augustine: the one word 'everlasting' binds both destinies, guarding against the evasion that the fire but not the punishment is eternal; endless pain is just, as law measures not by the time of the crime; he refutes the milder universalist views.
The plain parallelism — the same adjective for punishment and life — makes the everlastingness of the one the measure of the other.
Origen (named by Augustine) held the devil and his angels would at last be delivered from torment — a view the Church rebuked.

debates.question: Is the punishment of the wicked truly eternal?

sides.position: Eternal, as the parallel word for 'life' requires
At last ending, the lost (even the devil) finally restored

outliers.note: Held that the devil and his angels, after proportioned suffering, would be restored — the most 'merciful' of the views Augustine refutes.
